steps.prompt: Which word is the finite verb?

hints.impleta: 'impleta' is a perfect passive participle, not the finite verb

hints.to eat: 'to eat' is 'edere' — 'est' here is from 'esse' (to be)
hints.to go out: 'to go out' is 'exire' — 'est' is from 'esse' (to be)
hints.to fill: 'to fill' is 'implere' — 'est' is the auxiliary verb 'to be'

hints.present indicative: 'est' is present, but combined with the participle 'impleta' it forms the perfect passive
hints.imperfect: Imperfect would use 'erat' — 'est' + participle = perfect passive
hints.pluperfect: Pluperfect would use 'erat' + participle — 'est' + participle = perfect passive

hints.edo, esse, edi, esum: These are principal parts of 'edo' (to eat) — note 'esse' can mean 'to eat' too
hints.eo, ire, ivi, itum: These are principal parts of 'eo' (to go)
hints.fio, fieri, factus sum, —: These are principal parts of 'fio' (to become/be made)

steps.prompt: 'Impleta' is a participle. The infinitive of its verb translates to?

hints.to fill up a cup: 'implere' means 'to fulfill/fill' in a broad sense, not just filling a cup — but the meaning here is 'to fulfill'
hints.to employ: 'to employ' is not a Latin verb form here — 'impleta' comes from 'implere'

hints.imploro, implorare, imploravi, imploratu: These are principal parts of 'imploro' (to implore)
hints.impono, imponere, imposui, impositum: These are principal parts of 'impono' (to place upon)
hints.implicare, implicavi, implicatum, implic: These are principal parts of 'implico' (to enfold) — and in wrong order

hints.accusative: Accusative would be 'scripturam' — 'scriptura' is nominative, the subject
hints.ablative: Ablative would also be 'scriptura' in form, but here it is the subject (nominative)
hints.vocative: Vocative would also look like 'scriptura' but this is a statement, not an address

hints.2nd declension: 2nd declension nouns have genitive in -i — 'scripturae' ends in -ae
hints.3rd declension: 3rd declension nouns have genitive in -is — 'scripturae' ends in -ae
hints.4th declension: 4th declension nouns have genitive in -us — 'scripturae' ends in -ae

translation: Today this scripture has been fulfilled
